---
title: "Week 4: ANCOVA"
date: "2026-09-07"
---

## Overview

This week introduces analysis of covariance (ANCOVA) through the lens of multiple regression with dummy variables. You will learn how adding a continuous covariate to a group-comparison model adjusts for pre-existing differences, producing more precise and less biased estimates of group effects. Understanding ANCOVA also sets the stage for the broader multiple regression framework in later weeks.

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

- Create and interpret dummy-coded variables for categorical predictors
- Explain how ANCOVA adjusts group means for a continuous covariate
- Interpret regression coefficients in a model with both categorical and continuous predictors
- Describe the parallel regression lines assumption and its implications
- Identify situations where omitted variable bias threatens causal inference

## Readings

- {{< fa book-open >}} **Required**: [Chapter 2: ANCOVA]({{< var books.lecture_notes >}}/chapters/02-ancova.html){target="_blank"}

## R Lab

- {{< fa laptop-code >}} [Lab 03: ANOVA and ANCOVA in R]({{< var books.r_lab_book >}}/labs/lab03-anova-ancova.html){target="_blank"}

## Key Concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Dummy coding** | Representing a categorical variable with 0/1 indicator variables |
| **ANCOVA** | A model that compares group means after adjusting for one or more continuous covariates |
| **Adjusted means** | Group means recalculated as if all groups had the same covariate value |
| **Parallel lines** | The assumption that the covariate has the same slope in every group |
| **Confounding** | When a third variable is associated with both the predictor and the outcome |
| **Omitted variable bias (OVB)** | Bias in a coefficient caused by leaving a relevant variable out of the model |
